caveats: a: The source combines firm sizes and accounting settings rather than isolating NAICS 541211.
a: Vendor-reported efficiency is not a wage-weighted task census.
rho: The Deloitte poll covers finance and accounting, not only CPA firms.
rho: Current adoption is not a direct measure of five-year implementation.
e: No public source tabulates recurring-revenue eligibility at the specified EBITDA band.
e: Annual tax and audit engagements are treated as repeat service, although retention can vary by client.
s5: The sources show market activity and succession relevance, not the probability of a qualifying transfer for this precise firm population.
s5: Announced acquisitions and private-equity investments are not a census of LMM control transfers.
q: CAS is a subset of CPA-firm services and may have more recurring fixed-fee revenue than audit and tax practices.
q: Pricing form is an imperfect proxy for realized five-year benefit retention.
d5: Employment is not service quantity.
d5: The BLS projection includes all employing industries for the occupation rather than only CPA offices.
o: Neither source measures future operator-required service quantity for the frozen lens.
o: The result differs materially by client complexity and by tax, audit, bookkeeping, and advisory mix.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External businesses and households buy annual, monthly, quarterly, or repeat professional engagements. Revenue may be hourly, fixed-fee, subscription, value-priced, or a mix.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive finance units, shell entities, non-control investment vehicles, and firms whose work is solely nonrepeat project consulting are excluded.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. CPA firms serving external clients through recurring or repeat audit, tax, accounting, bookkeeping, payroll, client-accounting, and related advisory engagements, in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: S3 measures AI use cases and vendor-reported time savings across accounting, tax, audit, and advisory rather than the wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task mix of U.S. NAICS 541211 LMM firms. I map its evidence on reconciliations, transaction coding, document analysis, return preparation, research, reporting, and workflow triage to exposed task families, then bound for professional judgment, client interaction, and final review. This proxy can overstate exposure where audit and advisory wages dominate, and understate it where routine tax and bookkeeping work dominates.
caveats: The source combines firm sizes and accounting settings rather than isolating NAICS 541211.
Vendor-reported efficiency is not a wage-weighted task census.
a.change_evidence: Obtain anonymized time-and-billing extracts by service line from LMM CPA firms, paired with pre-and-post implementation staffing and throughput for tax, audit, bookkeeping, and CAS workflows.
a.rationale: The occupation is rich in structured documents, transactions, reconciliations, research, and recurring reporting. S3 documents automation and augmentation across those workflows, while retaining human review in audit and tax. The base treats a substantial share of current labor as exposed but not all professional work as substitutable.

d5.bridge: S2 projects national employment for accountants and auditors over a ten-year interval, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current NAICS 541211 service basket over five years. I use its occupation outlook and stated drivers as a directional demand proxy, with the base converted to a five-year midpoint. It can overstate service demand if productivity reduces purchased labor and understate demand from advisory, tax complexity, and recurring compliance work.
caveats: Employment is not service quantity.
The BLS projection includes all employing industries for the occupation rather than only CPA offices.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain constant-price service-line revenue and engagement-volume data from AICPA MAP panels, tax software providers, and audit-practice benchmarking for LMM CPA firms.
d5.rationale: BLS expects continued demand from economic activity, complex tax and regulation, and financial-record preparation and examination. The range is deliberately modest because the requested measure holds service quality and price constant while automation changes delivery.

caveats: No public source tabulates recurring-revenue eligibility at the specified EBITDA band.
Annual tax and audit engagements are treated as repeat service, although retention can vary by client.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level revenue survey that separates recurring, annual-repeat, and project-only revenue for NAICS 541211 firms in the LMM EBITDA band would directly test eligibility.
e.rationale: The Census definition centers on CPA establishments that audit and attest and permits related bookkeeping, tax preparation, and payroll processing. Those offerings are normally annual or ongoing external engagements. The deduction excludes the minority of LMM firms whose service mix is solely nonrepeat projects or lacks a transferable operating practice.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $68,045.5M / sectoral output (T30) $134,726.2M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $263,807 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541211); class <5: 35,096 firms, avg revenue/firm $300,013 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $46,952 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 9,158 firms, avg revenue/firm $944,405 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $147,799 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 4,257 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,000,227 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $313,036 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,015 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,602,856 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,033,347 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 271 firms, avg revenue/firm $37,129,554 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,810,775 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 88 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,137,349,114 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $177,995,136 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 2,286

o.bridge: S2 describes routine accounting automation alongside continued accountant demand, and S3 documents human review, professional judgment, and advisory roles in accounting. I translate these occupation and profession-wide statements into the share of the future service quantity still needing an accountable external CPA-firm operator. The result may be too high if low-complexity clients self-serve with software, and too low if regulation and assurance requirements preserve human accountability.
caveats: Neither source measures future operator-required service quantity for the frozen lens.
The result differs materially by client complexity and by tax, audit, bookkeeping, and advisory mix.
o.change_evidence: Measure client churn to self-service products and the share of completed engagements requiring licensed review, sign-off, or advisory contact across LMM CPA firms.
o.rationale: Routine preparation, coding, and reconciliation can be automated or self-served, but attest, tax, exception handling, advice, liability, and client trust preserve a large accountable-operator role. The base leaves material exposure to software substitution without assuming the profession disappears.

q.bridge: S5 measures pricing practices in client-accounting-services respondents, not the discounted five-year retained share of AI benefit across all NAICS 541211 services. I map fixed and subscription-style fees to temporary operator retention of efficiency gains, then reduce the result for fee review, scope management, hourly billing, competition, and client sharing. It may overstate retention where tax and audit fees are regularly repriced, and understate it for well-scoped fixed-fee recurring engagements.
caveats: CAS is a subset of CPA-firm services and may have more recurring fixed-fee revenue than audit and tax practices.
Pricing form is an imperfect proxy for realized five-year benefit retention.
q.change_evidence: Collect engagement letters, pricing schedules, annual realization reports, and post-automation renewal outcomes from a representative LMM CPA-firm sample by service line.
q.rationale: Fixed pricing supports retention of operating efficiencies between repricing events, while annual fee review and a remaining hourly component constrain durable capture. The base assumes meaningful, but incomplete, retention after customer and competitive sharing.

rho.bridge: S4 measures finance and accounting respondents' current agent use, stated five-year expectations, and adoption barriers; S3 documents the staged implementation requirements in accounting. I translate those broader finance-and-accounting signals to the share of the exposed opportunity that LMM CPA firms can operationally implement in five years. It may overstate implementation because CPA-client confidentiality and audit liability are stricter than some respondent settings, but may understate adoption as accounting-specific platforms mature.
caveats: The Deloitte poll covers finance and accounting, not only CPA firms.
Current adoption is not a direct measure of five-year implementation.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal AICPA or PCPS survey showing service-line deployment, workflow coverage, controls, and realized staffing changes at LMM CPA firms would materially narrow this estimate.
rho.rationale: Adoption is plausible across common workflows, but confidence, integration, data hygiene, privacy, controls, staff training, and human review constrain how much opportunity becomes operational. The base allows broad workflow use without assuming autonomous professional judgment.

caveats: The sources show market activity and succession relevance, not the probability of a qualifying transfer for this precise firm population.
Announced acquisitions and private-equity investments are not a census of LMM control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A transaction database matched to firm revenue or EBITDA, plus AICPA or state-society owner-age and succession-plan survey microdata, would permit a direct LMM five-year transfer estimate.
s5.rationale: The base is a bounded five-year judgment for qualifying control transfers among eligible LMM firms. It reflects an active consolidation and succession market, while recognizing that visible transactions skew toward larger platforms and many small practices continue independently or close without a transferable operation.

principal_driver: Repeat external accounting work has many structured workflows that can be automated or augmented while preserving a role for accountable professional review.
principal_weakness: The most consequential uncertainties are the LMM transfer rate and the speed at which pricing and self-service software pass AI benefits through to clients.
